(b) Transfer of a pension from an Indian to the Home Treasury is permitted only once.

Procedure in paying.

1021. A gratuity is paid in a single sum, and not by instalments.

1025. A pension is payable in India monthly on and after the first day of the following month.

1049. At the Home Treasury pensions are payable quarterly.

Payment in a Colony.

1051. The rules in this section apply to pensions granted under the rules in any Chapter of these regulations:—

1052. The pension of a pensioner residing in any Colony named in Appendix No. 17 may be paid there.

1057. Rate of Exchange.—Pensions stated in Indian money shall, where the standard of currency of the Colony is gold, except when it is otherwise arranged, be paid in sterling money at the rate of exchange annually fixed for the adjustment of transactions between the British and Indian Governments, subject to the condition that the rate of 1s. 9d. the rupee is fixed, with effect from the 1st of April, 1890, as the minimum rate at which the conversion into sterling shall be effected. If any payments are made at a different rate or otherwise erroneously, they should be adjusted in subsequent payments.

NOTE.—[See Notes to Article 1015A above.]

1058. Transfer of Payment.—In case a pensioner desires transfer of payment of his pension from one Colony to another, the Government of India will recognise the proceedings of the Colonial Authorities sanctioning such transfer, which should, however, be reported separately by the pensioner to the Government of India and to the Under-Secretary of State for India.

1059. Upon his return to India an officer should deliver up his duplicate Warrant, which will serve the purpose of a last-pay certificate.

CHAPTER XLV.

MEMBERS OF THE INDIAN CIVIL SERVICE.

Applications.

1062. The rules in this Section apply to all members of the Indian Civil Service.

1066. During leave to Europe.—(a) A Member of the Indian Civil Service, who wishes to retire from the service while on leave in Europe, must submit his application to the Secretary of State.

(b) When an officer makes his application under this Article, whether after completing his full period of service, or after having been declared by the Medical Board

to be unfit for further service in India, his resignation is accepted, subject to the adjustment of any demands that may be standing against him in India; and he is directed to apply to the Government of India, the Government of Madras, or the Government of Bombay, as the case may be, for the annuity or gratuity to which his length of service may entitle him.

Commencement of Annuities.

1068. The annuity of an officer who leaves India by sea, when retiring from the service at the end of subsi-

CHAPTER XLV—continued.

diary leave, begins, and his subsidiary leave ends, on the day of the departure of the vessel in which he sails.

NOTE.—[As soon as an officer gives over charge of his office, the Accountant General should furnish the following information to the Government of India in the Finance Department, if the officer is a Member of the Indian Civil Service on the Bengal Establishment, and to the Government of Madras or Bombay, if the officer is a Member of the Indian Civil Service on the Madras or Bombay Establishment:—

  1. (i) Name of officer.
  2. (ii) Date on which he made over charge of his office.
  3. (iii) The amount of subsidiary leave granted, if any.
  4. (iv) Date up to (and including) which subsidiary leave allowances have been drawn.
  5. (v) What demands, if any, are outstanding against the officer.

1069. The annuity of a Member of Council who has not previously resigned his seat in Council, or whose successor has not entered upon his office, commences from the day following that on which the vessel in which he leaves India sails, or from the expiry of his five years' tenure of office, whichever date is earlier.

Manner of Payment.

1071. Annuities are payable in arrear, quarterly, and to date of decease.

1072. (a) Payment of annuities and gratuities may be taken at the Home Treasury in sterling money, or in India in Government rupees, at the following rate:—

(i) If the annuitant was a member of the Indian Civil Service on the Bengal Establishment, 10½ Government rupees for each pound sterling.

(ii) If the annuitant was a member of the Indian Civil Service on the Madras or Bombay Establishment, 10·65 Government rupees for each pound sterling.

NOTE.—[The reasons for the difference in the rates of exchange are to be found in the history of the Annuity Funds. The rates of exchange differ considerably in the various Service Funds.]

(b) Provided that any annuitant, who is a permanent resident in India, may, if he wishes to draw his annuity in that country, exercise the option of receiving it at the rate of exchange annually fixed for the adjustment of transactions between the British and Indian Exchequers.

NOTE.—[This rule applies to all officers whose pensions are stated in sterling, and who, being resident in Asia, take payment in India.]

Transfer of Payment.

1073. Transfer from the Home Treasury to an Indian treasury, and vice versa, is permitted twice only.

Payment in a Colony.

1076. Payment of annuities may be made in any of the Colonies named in Appendix No. 17 in accordance with the procedure laid down in Chapter XLIV., Articles 1051 to 1061.

CHAPTER XLVI.CHAPLAINS.

1077. Applications may be made to, and pensions are granted either by the Government of India, the Government of Bombay, or the Government of Madras (as the case may be), or by the Secretary of State.

1. At least six months before a Chaplain, whether of the Church of England or the Church of Scotland, completes his full period of service, a statement should be furnished by the Local Government concerned to the Government of India in the Home Department, for transmission to the Secretary of State, showing the date on which the Chaplain completes such service, whether he is in England or in India. It should also be stated whether he is on duty or on leave, and if the latter, for what period.

1078. Applications in England.—(a) A Chaplain proceeding to England on retirement without applying for pension to the Government of India, the Government of Bombay, or the Government of Madras (as the case may be), should procure a certificate:—

  1. (i) If he is a Chaplain of the Bengal Establishment, or a Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in the Bengal Presidency;—from the Comptroller, Indian Treasuries;
  2. (ii) If he is a Chaplain on the Bombay or the Madras Establishment, or a Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in Madras or Bombay;—from the Accountant-General at Bombay or Madras (as the case may be);

showing his service, residence, the amount of pension to which he is entitled, and the date from which his pension may be paid.

1079. Applications in India.—A Chaplain belonging to the Bengal Ecclesiastical Establishment who wishes to obtain pension from the Authorities in India, must submit his application to the Government of India in the Home Department, through the Archdeacon or Bishop of his Diocese.

1080. A Chaplain belonging to the Madras or Bombay Ecclesiastical Establishment, must submit his application to the Government of Madras or the Government of Bombay, as the case may be, through the Archdeacon or the Bishop of his Diocese.

1081. A Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in the Bengal Presidency, who wishes to obtain pension from the Authorities in India, must submit his application to the Government of India in the Home Department, if he is not Senior Chaplain through the Senior Chaplain of the Church of Scotland.

1082. A Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in Madras or Bombay must submit his application to the Government of Madras or Bombay, as the case may be, if he is not Senior Chaplain, through the Senior Chaplain of his Presidency.

1083. The Home Department, before accepting the resignation of a Chaplain on the Bengal Establishment or a Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in the Bengal Presidency, should obtain a certificate from the Comptroller, Indian Treasuries, showing the Chaplain's service, residence, and the amount of pension to which he is entitled. The Government of Madras or Bombay, as the case may be, should obtain a like certificate from the Accountant-General of the Presidency concerned.

1084. Grant of Pension in India.—(a) In the case of the Bengal Presidency if the Chaplain's resignation is accepted, the case should be forwarded to the Finance Department of the Government of India, in which department a Resolution will be recorded granting the pension to which the Chaplain may be entitled.

(b) In the case of the Madras or Bombay Presidency the procedure is the same, but the Resolution is issued by Madras or Bombay, as the case may be.

1085. Last-pay Certificate.—A Chaplain to whom pension has been granted in India should be careful before proceeding to England to obtain the usual certificate of the last issue of pay or pension to him in India.

EXTRACT FROM CHAPTER I.

REGULATIONS RELATING TO TRAVELLING ALLOWANCES ON JOINING FIRST APPOINTMENT OF CERTAIN OFFICERS APPOINTED IN EUROPE.

1195. The rules regarding—

(a) passage to India and outfit allowances (in case of appointment in Europe).

(b) return to Europe (on termination of appointment), of certain high officers, which have been framed by Her Majesty's Secretary of State for India, are given in Appendix 29.

1196. Officers appointed in Europe to the Telegraph, Public Works, Forest, and Educational Departments, to the Geological Survey of India, and to the Bengal Pilot Service, are ordinarily allowed by the Secretary of State a free passage to India.

Note.—[The rule regarding a Chief Justice or Judge of any of the High Courts is included in Statutory Rule No. 41 in Article 600.]

1197. Chaplains also are ordinarily allowed a free passage to India, and in the contracts now entered into by them they engage to refund the cost of their passage to India, in the event of their resigning their appointments within three years after the date of their arrival in India on grounds other than ill-health under a Medical certificate.

1197A. If the health of a Chaplain appointed on probation compels him to leave India during the probationary period, he must resign the service entirely, in which case he will not be entitled to anything beyond a free return passage to his country.

A probationer removed from the service for misconduct of any kind, or who resigns his position for any cause other than certified ill-health, will not be entitled to a return passage to his country, and will be bound to refund the cost of his passage to India.

1198. An officer appointed to the Bengal Pilot Service is granted an Outfit Allowance of £30.

1200. An Officer, who is appointed by the Secretary of State, while resident in Europe, and who is not one of the high officers mentioned in Article 1195, is entitled to Travelling Allowance at the rates laid down in Chapter XI, VIII. from the capital town of the Presidency to which he is attached, to the first station to which he is posted.

1201. (a) If such an officer disembarks in India at any port other than the capital town of the Presidency to which he is attached, he is entitled to Travelling Allowance from such port to the first station to which he is posted, limited to the amount to which he would have been entitled under the preceding Article if he had disembarked at such capital town.

(b) But if an officer is directed by the Secretary of State to proceed to a particular port, he is entitled to Travelling Allowance from that port.

1202. For the purposes of the preceding Articles an officer attached to any Province or Presidency, other than Bombay or Madras, is held to be attached to the Bengal Presidency.

PART XII. APPENDICES.

APPENDIX No. 17.

Referred to in Articles 936 and 1052.

LIST OF COLONIAL TREASURERS, DISTRICT PAYMASTERS, AND OTHER IMPERIAL AGENTS IN THE COLONIES.

Station. Designation of Agent.
Bahamas. The Receiver-General and Treasurer, Nassau, Bahamas
Barbados (including British Guiana, Trinidad, &c.) The District Paymaster, Barbados.
Bermuda. The District Paymaster, Bermuda.
British Columbia The Naval Storekeeper, Esquimalt Yard (British Columbia).
British Honduras The Colonial Treasurer, Belize, British Honduras.
Dominion of Canada (except British Columbia and Newfoundland) The District Paymaster, Halifax, Nova Scotia.
Cape of Good Hope The District Paymaster, Cape Town.
Ceylon The District Paymaster, Ceylon.
Cyprus The District Paymaster, Cyprus.
Falkland Islands The Colonial Treasurer, Falkland Islands.
Fiji Islands The Governor, Fiji Islands.
Gambia The Colonial Treasurer, Gambia, West Africa.
Gibraltar The District Paymaster, Gibraltar.
Gold Coast The Colonial Treasurer, Accra, West Africa.
Hong-Kong The District Paymaster, Hong-Kong.
Jamaica The District Paymaster, Jamaica.
Lagos The Colonial Treasurer, Lagos, West Africa.
Malta The District Paymaster, Malta.
Mauritius The District Paymaster, Mauritius.
Natal The District Paymaster, Pietermaritzburg, Natal.
New South Wales The Colonial Treasurer, Sydney, New South Wales.
New Zealand The Colonial Treasurer, Wellington, New Zealand. Included in the Dominion of Canada (see above).
Nova Scotia The Colonial Treasurer, Brisbane, Queensland.
Queensland The Acting District Paymaster, Sierra Leone, West Africa.
Sierra Leone The Colonial Treasurer, Adelaide, South Australia.
South Australia The Acting District Paymaster, St. Helena.
St. Helena The District Paymaster, Straits Settlements.
Straits Settlements The Agent for Imperial Expenditure, Hobart, Tasmania.
Tasmania The District Paymaster, Pietermaritzburg, Natal.
Transvaal The Naval Storekeeper, Esquimalt Yard (British Columbia).
Vancouver's Island. The Colonial Treasurer, Melbourne, Victoria.
Victoria The Colonial Treasurer, Perth, Western Australia.
Western Australia

APPENDIX No. 29.

Referred to in Article 1195.

RULES made under the Statute 43 Vict., ch. 3, by the Secretary of State for India in Council, fixing the Allowances for Equipment and Voyage of the Governor-General of India, Members of Council of the Governor-General of India, Governors of Madras and Bombay, Commanders-in-Chief of the Forces in India, Commanders-in-Chief of Madras and Bombay, Bishops of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay. Dated 3rd of January, 1888.

I.

1. The allowance for equipment and voyage of a Governor General of India, resident in Europe at the time of his appointment, is fixed at £3,500.

2. Except as declared in these Rules, Government will make no arrangements for, and will defray none of the expenses connected with the conveyance or passage of a Governor-General, his family, or suite, to or from India.

3. A newly appointed Governor General proceeding to India from Europe to enter upon his office may be accompanied by three combatant military officers for his personal staff, the expenses of whose passage will be defrayed by Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department as to military officers proceeding to India on duty.

4. For an ex-Governor General returning to Europe, on resignation, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be gratuitously provided, to convey him and his family and suite to Suez; but the homeward voyage of the ship thus provided shall not be continued beyond Suez. The ex-Governor General will bear the cost of entertainment and messing incurred while on board, in respect of himself and his party, according to the rules in force relating to the conveyance of passengers in Indian Marine troopships.

5. No grant from Indian revenues will be made to any ex-Governor General for his homeward journey from Suez.

6. An ex-Governor General returning to Europe may be accompanied by two officers of his personal staff, for whom free passages to England will be allowed according to the rules in the Military Department, and also free return passages to India, if they start on their return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.

7. In the event of a Governor General dying and leaving a widow in India, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be provided to convey her to Suez in like manner as in the case of an ex-Governor General. The widow of a deceased Governor General so returning to Europe may be accompanied by one officer of the personal staff on the conditions mentioned in the preceding rule, and shall bear all such expenses as would, in ordinary course, be borne by an ex-Governor General returning to Europe.

II.

8. The allowances for equipment and voyage of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, the Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India, of Madras, and of Bombay, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

£
The Governor of Madras ..... 1,000
The Governor of Bombay ..... 1,000
The Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India ..... 500
The Commander-in-Chief, Madras..... 500
The Commander-in-Chief, Bombay ... 500

9. A Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India proceeding from Europe to assume his command may be accompanied by three combatant military officers for his personal staff, and newly-appointed Governors of Madras and of Bombay, and Commanders-in-Chief of Madras and of Bombay proceeding from Europe to take up their duties, may respectively be accompanied by two combatant military officers for their personal staff, the passages of these officers being provided at the expense of Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department as to military officers proceeding to India on duty; but with these exceptions, Government will make no arrangements for, and will defray none of the expenses connected with, the passage or conveyance of any of the officers mentioned in Rule 8, or of their families, to or from India.

10. An ex-Governor of Madras or Bombay, or an ex-Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India or of Madras or Bombay, or the widow of a Governor or of a Commander-in-Chief who may die in India leaving a widow there, returning to Europe, may be accompanied by one officer of his personal staff, for whom a free passage will be allowed by Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department, and also a free return passage if he starts on the return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.

III.

11. The allowances for equipment and voyage of Members of the Council of the Governor General of India, and of Bishops of Calcutta, of Madras, and of Bombay, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

£
Members of the Council of the Governor General of India..... 300
The Bishop of Calcutta..... 300
The Bishop of Madras ..... 300
The Bishop of Bombay ..... 300

12. An officer of any of the Indian Services, temporarily residing in Europe, or a military officer transferred from one superior command in India to another during temporary residence out of India, shall not be deemed to be resident in Europe at the time of appointment, within the meaning of Rule 8 or Rule 11.

IV.

RULES made under Statute 24 and 25 Vict., Ch. 104, Sect. 6, by the Secretary of State in Council of India, fixing the expenses for equipment and voyage of the Chief Justice and Judges of the several High Courts established under the said Statute, dated the 31st January, 1888.

39. For the purposes of defraying the expenses of equipment and voyage from Europe on first appointment there shall be allowed—

To a Chief Justice or Judge of any High Court ..... £300
But no such allowance shall be made to any person who, being in India, is appointed to the office of Chief Justice or Judge, or who, having been in India, is in Europe at the time of his appointment with the intention of returning to India.

Referred to in the Note to Article 600, Section III, Rule 40.

EXTRACT FROM THE OLD STATUTORY RULES REGARDING THE PENSIONS OF HIGH COURT JUDGES.

Section III. Pensions.

Chief Justices.

27. A Chief Justice of the High Court at Calcutta after an actual service of eleven and a half years as Judge of a High Court, of which period at least five years and nine months shall have been as Chief Justice of the High Court at Calcutta, shall receive a pension not exceeding £1,800 per annum.

28. A Chief Justice of the High Court at Madras and Bombay, and for the North-Western Provinces, respectively, after an actual service of eleven and a half-years as Judge of a High Court, of which period at least five years and nine months shall have been as Chief Justice, shall receive a pension not exceeding £1,500 per annum.

Judges.

29. A Judge of a High Court not being eligible for pension at a higher rate under Rule 27 or 28, shall, after an actual service of eleven and a half years as Judge, receive a pension not exceeding £1,200 per annum.

Invalid Pensions.

30. A Chief Justice or Judge who retires on medical certificate after six years and nine months' actual service shall receive a pension not exceeding one-half the amount of pension allowed for the full period of service.

Previous Service.

31. In the event of a Judge receiving a pension under these Rules, he will not be entitled to any other pension or retiring allowance.

32. When a Judge who at the time of his appointment to a High Court was a member of one of the Government Services in India, is permitted to retire before completing the full period of service qualifying him for pension as a Judge of a High Court, he shall receive such a pension as he might receive under the rules applicable to the branch of the service to which he belonged when so appointed, reckoning the period of service as a Judge of a High Court towards service for that pension.

REGULATIONS FOR ADMISSION TO THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS.*

1. A certain number of appointments to the Indian Staff Corps will be offered to candidates for admission to the Royal Military College at each Further Examination, and to officers of the Militia (not over 23 years of age) at each Competitive Examination.

2. All Queen's cadets (British and Indian) and Honorary Queen's cadets nominated by the Secretary of State for India in Council will have the option of electing whether they will join the Royal Military College for appointment to the Staff Corps, or for Commissions in British Cavalry or Infantry. The appointments in the Staff Corps remaining after the claims of the Queen's Cadets and Honorary Queen's Cadets (Indian) have been satisfied will be allotted in order of merit to successful candidates who have elected for the Indian Staff Corps at the Sandhurst Further and Militia Competitive Examinations.

3. Election shall be final, and no candidate for the Staff Corps will thereafter be eligible for transfer to the Cavalry or Infantry of the Line except by exchange.

4. After the final examination at the Royal Military College, and in the case of Militia candidates after the Competitive Examination, a successful candidate will be gazetted as Second Lieutenant on the unattached List of the British Army, and will be provided with passage to India. He will be required to embark within two months of being gazetted. From the date of first commission to the date of arrival in India he will receive the British pay of his rank.

5. On arrival in India he will be attached to a British regiment, and will receive Indian pay and allowances as an officer of the British Army. While so attached, leave (excepting privilege leave) may only be granted to him on medical certificate, and during any leave out of India he will be restricted to the British pay of his rank.

6. At the expiration of one year's actual duty with a British regiment (exclusive of all leave excepting privilege leave) he will be admitted to the Staff Corps in the rank of Second Lieutenant and appointed to a Native regiment.

7. At the expiration of one year's actual duty with a Native regiment (exclusive of all leave excepting privilege leave) he will be promoted to the rank of Lieutenant, provided he has passed the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard. Should he not then have passed the examination he will only be promoted from the date of passing. An exception to this rule may be made, with the sanction of the Secretary of State for India, in the case of decay or illness resulting from field service.

8. He will not be permitted to remain in the Service unless during the first three years from date of first commission his retention therein is shown to be in very respect desirable.

9. Within three years of the date of his admission to the Staff Corps he will be required to pass the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under Indian regulations.

10. Until he shall have passed the above examinations he will not be eligible to hold any permanent or officiating appointment (regimental or other) except that of Squadron or Wing Officer.

11. Should he not pass the examinations within the period of three years, he will be removed from the Service and be provided with passage to Europe; he may also be allowed, at the discretion of the Government of India, a gratuity not exceeding one year's British pay of his rank.

12. No extension of the period within which the examinations must be passed will be allowed except on account of delay or illness resulting from employment on field service, and only then with the special sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

13. Any officers who may from time to time be required to supplement the direct supply from Sandhurst, will be drawn from the Cavalry and Infantry of the Line, and the Royal Artillery. Also, any officer of the British Army who may show exceptional qualifications for service in India, may, with the concurrence of the Secretary of State for War, be specially selected for the Indian Staff Corps. Officers appointed under this paragraph will be transferred permanently to the Staff Corps, and will not be permitted to revert to their British regiments, except by exchange under paragraph 14.

* These regulations will in the first instance apply to candidates for admission to the Royal Military College who pass the Further Examination in June, 1891, and to the Militia officers who pass the Competitive Examination for commissions in the Army in September 1892. Officers admitted to the Army before the 1st September, 1892, will remain eligible for appointment to the Staff Corps, to such extent as may be determined, under the regulations which follow (see page 5).

† As amended by Army Order No. 173 of September, 1892.

14. An exchange may be permitted between an officer of the British Service and an officer of the Staff Corps, provided both officers have under seven years' service, that the officer exchanging into the Staff Corps is fully qualified for staff employ under Indian regulations, that immediate and suitable employment is available for him, and that he is physically fit for service in India.

15. Under special circumstances or in special cases the Secretary of State for India in Council may deviate from any of the above regulations.

ADMISSION OF OFFICERS ADMITTED TO THE ARMY BEFORE THE 1st SEPTEMBER, 1892.

PART I.

Officers serving in India.

1. An officer serving in India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:—

  1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty whether in or out of India.
  2. II. He shall not, at date of appointment to a Native regiment, be more than 25 years of age.

2. His application must be accompanied by certificates in the Forms A 1 or A 2 and B, and by a declaration, in duplicate, in the Form C.

3. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops, and the date of joining a Native Corps shall be the date of appointment on probation.

4. Should the officer not have passed the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard before joining a Native Corps, he will be required to do so within 18 months of so joining, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.

5. After the expiration of one year from the date of appointment on probation, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.

6. On the expiration of 18 months from the date of appointment on probation he will be admitted to the Staff Corps whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms B, C and D.

7. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of appointment on probation) within which to pass.

8. Should he not pass within this period he will be removed from the Service.

PART II.

Officers of Corps serving out of India.*

9. An officer of a corps serving out of India will be eligible to become a Probationer on the following conditions:

  1. I. He shall, at time of application, have completed one year's actual regimental duty; in the case of officers of the West India regiment, two years.
  2. II. He shall not, on the 1st of January of the trooping season during which he embarks for India, be more than 25 years of age.

10.† His application must be submitted through his commanding officer to the Military Secretary, Horse Guards, and be accompanied by certificates in the Forms B and E, and by a declaration in the Form C.

11. If accepted, a candidate will be required to embark for India within two months of being so warned, and the date of embarkation for India shall be the date of his coming on the Indian Establishment.

12. The period of probation must be passed with Native troops; the candidate will be appointed to a Native Corps on first arrival in India, and the date of joining it will be the date of his appointment on probation.

13. Before the expiration of 18 months from date of joining a Native Corps, he must pass the examination in the Native languages by the lower standard, otherwise he will cease to be a Probationer.

14. After the expiration of one year from date of joining a Native Corps, the Probationer, provided he has passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation, may be admitted to the Staff Corps.

15. On the expiration of 18 months from the date of joining a Native Corps, he will be admitted to the Staff Corps, whether he has passed the above examinations or not, on production of certificates in the Forms B, C and D.

16. Should he not have passed the above examinations before admission to the Staff Corps, he will be allowed a further period of 18 months (or three years in all from date of joining a Native Corps) within which to pass.

17. Should he not pass within this period, he will be removed from the Service.

PART III.

General Regulations.

18. The names of Probationers will be retained on the lists of their British regiments until they are admitted to the Staff Corps.

19. During the period of probation it will be open to the Commanding Officer of a Native Corps to report that he thinks the Officer, either from temper or other cause, is unsuited for employment with Native troops; in which case the Commander-in-Chief in India, should he be satisfied that such is the case, will post the Officer to his own regiment or to a battalion of it, if one be serving in India, or, if not, to any regiment or battalion in India pending orders from His Royal Highness Commander-in-Chief.‡

20. The same course will be followed in the case of an officer who may not, at the expiration of 18 months' duty, be able to obtain the necessary certificates for admission to the Staff Corps.

* These Regulations are also applicable generally to officers of the Royal Marines.

† The application of an officer of Marines must be addressed to the Admiralty.

‡ An officer of Royal Marines ceasing to be a Probationer will not be attached to a British Regiment in India, but will at once be provided with passage to England, and will revert to the disposal of the Admiralty on arrival in that country.

21. No Officer will, be allowed to resign his probationary appointment except on sufficient grounds to be approved by the Commander-in-Chief in India, the Government of India, the Secretary of State for India in Council, and his Royal Highness the Commander-in-Chief. An officer applying for permission to resign will be required to produce a declaration and a certificate in the Forms F and G, and the Commander-in-Chief in India, if satisfied with the reason given, will at once post the officer to his own regiment or to a battalion of it, if one be serving in India, or, if not, to any regiment or battalion of the line in that country, his resignation being sent forward at once for the final order of the Secretary of State for India and His Royal Highness the Commander-in-Chief.

22. A Probationer obtaining leave out of India either on private affairs, or for more than six months on medical certificate, will be held to have resigned his probationary appointment.

23. Until the completion of three years' service a Probationer shall remain subject to the provisions of the Royal Warrant of 5 Dec. 1888.*

24. When an Officer is ultimately admitted to the Staff Corps the date assigned to his admission shall be that of his first appointment on probation.

25. An Officer shall not be eligible to hold any permanent or officiating appointment, regimental or other (except that of Squadron or Wing Officer), until he shall have passed the higher standard examination in the Native languages and the professional examination required under regulation.

26. No extension of the prescribed period within which these examinations must be passed will be allowed except on account of illness or delay resulting from employment on field service, and only then with the special sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

27. An Officer removed from the Service under para. 8 or para. 17 will receive a free passage to Europe and a gratuity of one year's English pay of his rank.

28. Under special circumstances, or in special cases, the Secretary of State for India in Council may deviate from any of the above regulations.

CERTIFICATES AND DECLARATIONS.

A 1.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India.
(Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant [of the] regiment, a candidate or the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed [years'] service from the date of his first commission, of which [years] were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant [has passed an examination in the native languages by the higher (or lower) standard, and] has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or† Artillery) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop, or battery) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant [has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

A 2.

Commanding Officer's Certificate.—Alternative which may be used for appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving in India of an Officer of short service. (Para. 2.)

Certified that Lieutenant [of the] regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed [years'] service from the date of his first commission, of which [years] were (or the whole period was, as the case may be), spent in India.

Lieutenant [has passed an examination in the Native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard] has passed his drills, and has performed in a satisfactory manner his duties as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or† Artillery). He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant [has served with this regiment he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

B.

Medical Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer and for admission. Paras. 2, 6, 10, and 15.)

I certify that to the best of my belief Lieutenant [is in good bodily health, and apparently well capable of undergoing the vicissitudes of service in India, that his vision is equal to the standard required for officers on entering the service, and that there is no defect in his hearing that would be likely to interfere with the proper performance of his duties as an officer of Cavalry (or Infantry).

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the medical officer of the regiment.)

* "An officer shall not be permitted to remain in Our Army unless during the first three years of his service his retention therein is known to be in every respect desirable."

† To be inserted if the Officer has so passed.

‡ Horse, field, or garrison, as the case may be.

C.Declaration of Candidate.—For appointment as Probationer. (Paras. 2, 6, 10, and 15.)

I hereby declare, upon my honour as an Officer and a gentleman, that I have not, nor to the best of my belief has any person on my behalf or in my interest, directly or indirectly, received or agreed to receive any sum of money, valuable security, or other thing in respect of my appointment to the Indian Staff Corps; and I promise, honourably and unreservedly that I will not receive the same or in any manner recognize or benefit by its receipt hereafter.

(Signature of candidate.)

[The above declaration must be drawn up in the officer's own handwriting, and must be read over in the presence of the officer commanding the regiment, who will attach his certificate as witness to the declaration in the following manner:—]

18 Read over in my presence, and signed before me this _____ day of _____

(Signature of commanding officer.)

[The declaration must also be subscribed by a second witness, who may be either an officer of standing in the service, or a civilian, being a householder.]

D.Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For admission to the Staff Corps. (Paras. 6 and 15.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission, of which _____ years were (or the whole period of which was, as the case may be) spent in India.

Lieutenant _____ has passed an examination in the native languages by the Higher (or Lower) standard, and has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an officer of Infantry (or Cavalry) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act, of the Native Articles of War, and of the Queen's Regulations, as well as of the India Army Regulations, besides having gained some general acquaintance with the organization and nature of all branches of the Indian Army.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant _____ has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duties, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature _____ he officer commanding the regiment.)

E.Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For appointment as Probationer from a Regiment serving out of India. (Para. 10.)

Certified that Lieutenant _____ of the _____ Regiment, a candidate for the Indian Staff Corps, has on this date completed _____ years' service from the date of his first commission.

Lieutenant _____ has attained such a knowledge of his drill and duty as an Officer of Infantry (or Cavalry, or Artillery) in all its branches as to qualify him for the command of a company (or troop, or battery) in all situations. He also possesses a fair knowledge of the Army Act of 1881, of the Army (Annual) Act and of the Queen's Regulations.

I further certify that during the period Lieutenant _____ has served with this regiment, he has been attentive to his duty, and that his conduct has been in all respects unexceptionable.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of the officer commanding the regiment.)

F.Declaration of Candidate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 21.)

I hereby solemnly declare, on my honour as an officer and a gentleman, that my resignation of my probationary appointment for the Staff Corps does not originate in any cause affecting my honour, or my character, or professional efficiency

(Station and date.)

(Signature of probationer.)

G.Commanding Officer's Certificate.—For resignation of probationary appointment. (Para. 21.)

I hereby certify that the resignation by Lieutenant _____ of his probationary appointment in the regiment under my command does not originate in any cause affecting his honour, or his fitness to continue in Her Majesty's service.

(Station and date.)

(Signature of commanding officer.)

EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE LOWER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination:—

BENGAL AND BOMBAY.
  1. (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy not less than an ordinary octavo page of the Bágh-o-Bahár.
  2. (b.) Translating accurately and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary octavo page of plain English into language similar to that of the Bágh-o-Bahár, in the Persian character.
  3. (c.) Reading fairly and translating readily and correctly a Hindustani manuscript written in the Persian character.
  4. (d.) (i.) Conversing with the examiner with fluency and with such correctness of pronunciation, grammar, and idiom as to be at once intelligible.
    (ii.) Conversing as above in the presence of the examiner with a native of the country.
MADRAS.
  1. (a.) Reading fairly, and construing with accuracy, not less than half an octavo page of the undermentioned work, the passages being selected by the examining officer himself before the examination—
    1. Bágh-o-Bahár, the second darvesh, including the story of King Azád Bakt.
  2. (b.) Conversing with an educated Native in the presence of the examiner on subjects likely to occur in the performance of regimental or professional duty, in the transaction of ordinary business, or in the course of every-day life.
EXAMINATION IN THE NATIVE LANGUAGES BY THE HIGHER STANDARD.

The following are the tests laid down for this examination:—

BENGAL AND BOMBAY.
  1. (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy, not less than an ordinary octavo page of the selections from the prose of the Prem Ságar.
  2. (b.) Translating accurately, and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary octavo page of plain English into language similar to that of the Prem Ságar in the Hindi character.
  3. (c.) Reading fairly and translating readily and correctly Hindustani manuscripts written in the Hindi character.
  4. (d.) Vivá voce translation into the Oriental language of a paper of English sentences, and conversing with the examiners, or in their presence with persons selected by them, with fluency and such correctness of pronunciation, grammar, and idiom as to be at once intelligible.
MADRAS.
  1. (a.) Reading fluently and construing with readiness and accuracy, not less than an ordinary octavo page of the Bágh-o-Bahár.
  2. (b.) Translating accurately, and with correctness of idiom and grammar, not less than half an ordinary page of plain English into the Hindustani language.
  3. (c.) Reading fairly, and translating readily and correctly, Hindustani manuscripts.
  4. (d.) Translating from English into Hindustani a paper of short sentences, the greater part on professional subjects, to be rendered accurately and idiomatically.
  5. (e.) Translating vivá voce into Hindustani the proceedings of a court-martial—a duty that will frequently be required of the candidates. (In translating the charge, finding, and sentence, the candidate will be allowed the use of the record; the evidence will be read out by the examiner and translated by the candidate to the natives in attendance. The examiners should satisfy themselves that the candidate's interpretation is clearly and correctly understood.)
  6. (f.) Conversing with natives with fluency, and with such correctness of pronunciation, grammar and idiom as to be at once intelligible, and in like manner to understand readily all that the natives say.
PROFESSIONAL EXAMINATION FOR ADMISSION TO THE STAFF CORPS.

The following are the tests for admission to the Staff Corps in virtue of qualifying appointments in Native regiments:—

  1. (a.) The evolutions of a regiment of cavalry or a battalion of infantry (as the case may be) as laid down in the regulations of those services respectively, including skirmishing, duties of outposts, patrols, escorts, advanced and rear-guards.
  2. (b.) The charge of a troop, company (as the case may be), or detachment in every position in which it may be placed, musketry instruction, requisitions, returns, and correspondence.
  3. (c.) The Indian articles of war and the Army Regulations, India, particularly all such as affect the conduct of native troops, their pay, pension, clothing, &c.
  4. (d.) The system of regimental interior economy pursued in the Native cavalry and infantry, and especially in

the regiment to which the officer has been attached, including the mode of investigating and dealing with offences, complaints, and petitions from the men, method of keeping rosters for furlough and guard, deductions made from the men's pay and system of accounts, together with a knowledge of every article of equipment used by the men, their cost, and the mode of carrying them.

In the case of probationers for the Native cavalry the examination will include the additional subjects under head (e).

(e.) System of purchase of horses, and the districts whence they are usually obtained, their prevailing defects and good qualities, stable management, general knowledge of farriery, and the diseases and injuries to which horses are liable, with their treatment.

The committee will satisfy themselves by examination in the field and viva voce questions that the officer is instructed in the required subjects.

EXAMINATION FOR REGIMENTAL AND ARMY STAFF APPOINTMENTS.*

Officers of the Staff Corps are not qualified to hold the appointments of Commandant, Second in Command, or Wing Commander, nor to leave their regiments for the purpose of holding any appointment whatever, except that of aide-de-camp (unless they come within the provisions of the following paragraphs), until they shall have passed an examination in the following subjects:—

PART I.

(a.)—Cavalry—

In the field:—

Command of a regiment in the following circumstances —

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Manceuvring on varied ground (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

(b.)—Infantry—

Equitation.

In the field:—

Command of a battalion in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Skirmishing (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

PART II.

(a.) Military law.

(b.) Tactics.

(c.) Field fortification.

(d.) Military topography.

Officers may be nominated at any time to staff appointments for distinguished service in the field, although they may not have passed the qualifying examination; but officers so appointed, who are liable to examination under this order, will be required to qualify within a reasonable time.

All officers in the Staff Corps who may have passed the final examination at the Staff College, will be exempted from the whole of the tests laid down.

PROMOTION.

Under Royal Warrant promotion in the Staff Corps is governed by such regulations as the Secretary of State for India may make from time to time.

The existing regulations are—

The promotion of Second Lieutenants from the Unattached List is governed by Rule 7 of the Regulations for Admission (page 4);

Second Lieutenants appointed to the Army before 1st September, 1892, are promoted to the rank of Lieutenant on permanent transfer to the Staff Corps;

Officers after 11 years' service, of which four must have been in the Staff Corps, become Captains;

After 20 years' service, of which six must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Majors; and

After 26 years' service, of which eight must have been in the Staff Corps, they become Lieutenant-Colonels.

The periods of four, six, and eight years' service in the Staff Corps are exclusive of all leave of absence except privilege leave and leave rendered necessary by wounds or illness contracted on service in the field.

* The qualifications required and examinations to be passed for the judge advocate general's department, the administrative departments of the army, the survey department, and for civil and political employ, will be found in the Indian Army Regulations.

A Lieutenant-Colonel becomes eligible for promotion to the rank of Colonel in the Army after three years' satisfactory service with that rank. Selection for promotion will be made under such regulations as the Secretary of State in Council may lay down.

Officers above the rank of Lieutenant are also eligible for promotion by brevet as a reward for distinguished service in the field, or for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

Promotion to the ranks of Major-General and Lieutenant-General will only be made by selection to fill appointments, or as a reward for distinguished service. There will be an establishment of 3 Generals.

Officers in civil employ, after ten years' absence from military duty, are removed from the effective list of the army, and placed on a supernumerary list, rising thereon, under the regulations in force, to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, but to no higher rank.

This condition is, however, not applicable to officers of rank taken for high political or civil employment, or to officers taken for temporary civil or political duties in the field or in newly-acquired territories.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

The claims to pension of the widows and families of officers of the Staff Corps (excluding those who entered the Staff Corps from the late Indian Army) are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pension to the widows and families of officers of H. M.'s British Forces, as may be in force for the time being.

The following are the rates of ordinary pension:—

Pension to Widow. Compassionate Allowance to each Child.
Yearly.
£		 Yearly.
£
General Officer, including Brigadier-General 120 20
Lieutenant-Colonel or Brevet-Colonel 90 16
Major 70 14
Captain 50 12
Lieutenant 40 10

with increased pensions in the case of the widows and children of officers who have been killed in action or have died of wounds or illness contracted on field service.

Compassionate allowances to sons cease at 18 years of age, and to daughters at 21 years of age or marriage.

Widows and families are also entitled to pensions from the Indian Military Service Family Pension Fund, to which all officers must subscribe as a condition of their appointment to the Staff Corps.
